serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chilli oil condiment is approximately one teaspoon (5 g), used as a dipping sauce or flavour enhancer alongside dumplings, noodles, or rice dishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chili oil, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Oils, chili
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No branded Din Tai Fung Taiwan Chilli Oil entry exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for chilli-infused oil (predominantly vegetable/sesame oil base with chilli solids). Energy and fat values reflect the oil-dominant composition. Sodium is estimated from typical commercial chilli oil formulations reported in USDA Branded Food entries for comparable chilli oil products. Values represent a best-estimate composite for a chilli oil condiment of this type.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fats and oil-based condiments — analogous estimate based on fat-dominant foods with negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chilli oil in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Chilli oil is a fat-dominant condiment with very low carbohydrate content (approximately 4.5 g per 100 g, predominantly non-glycaemic). GI is estimated at 10 (low category) by analogy with other fat-dominant, low-carbohydrate condiments and oils documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 5 g serving = (10 × 0.225) / 100 = 0.02, rounded to 0.2 for practical reporting. The food has negligible glycaemic impact in typical serving quantities.

hypertension.guidance: Din Tai Fung Taiwan Chilli Oil contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should limit use to small amounts (one teaspoon or less per serving) to avoid excessive sodium accumulation, particularly when combined with other high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and negligible glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a low estimated glycaemic index, chilli oil used in typical condiment quantities (5 g per serving) has negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is generally acceptable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, though total caloric intake from the high fat content should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat and saturated fat content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chilli oil is energy-dense (approximately 820 kcal per 100 g) with approximately 13 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should restrict use to small condiment portions and prefer formulations with a higher ratio of monounsaturated to saturated fat, noting that the predominant fat in this product is monounsaturated (approximately 52 g per 100 g), which is relatively favourable compared to saturated-fat-dominant oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Din Tai Fung Taiwan Chilli Oil (豐味辣油) is a premium bottled chilli oil condiment produced by Din Tai Fung, the internationally acclaimed Taiwanese restaurant chain renowned for its xiao long bao (soup dumplings). Sold in 160 g glass jars, this product is a signature retail offering available at Din Tai Fung outlets in Singapore and other markets. The oil is crafted by infusing high-quality vegetable and sesame oil with dried chillies, garlic, and a proprietary blend of spices, resulting in a deep amber-red oil with a fragrant, mildly spicy, and nutty flavour profile. The texture is smooth and oily with fine chilli sediment that settles at the bottom of the jar. It is used as a dipping condiment for dumplings, wontons, and noodles, or as a finishing oil drizzled over rice, congee, or cold dishes. Nutritionally, chilli oil is a fat-dominant condiment providing approximately 820 kcal per 100 g, with about 90 g total fat, predominantly monounsaturated fatty acids. Carbohydrate content is low at approximately 4.5 g per 100 g, making it glycaemically negligible in typical serving sizes. Sodium content of approximately 320 mg per 100 g warrants moderation for individuals with hypertension. The product contains sesame as a declared allergen. It is suitable for vegetarians and vegans. Clinically, the primary concerns are caloric density and sodium content; the low carbohydrate load makes it broadly compatible with diabetic dietary patterns when used in small quantities.

confidence_reason: No branded Din Tai Fung Taiwan Chilli Oil entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for chilli-infused oil and comparable USDA Branded Food entries for commercial chilli oil products. The food is a well-characterised condiment type (fat-dominant chilli oil) and the macro profile is consistent with the product category, but exact formulation values for this specific branded product are not available from an approved source. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chilli oil / oils with chilli) and comparable USDA Branded Food entries for commercial chilli oil products, as no approved primary source entry exists for this specific branded product. 2. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy chilli oil reference entry. 3. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy chilli oil reference entry. 4. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy chilli oil reference entry. 5. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy chilli oil reference entry. 6. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy chilli oil reference entry. 7. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy chilli oil reference entry. 8. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as chilli oil is a plant-derived product containing no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero by product category. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as chilli oil is a plant-derived condiment; naturally zero for this food category. 10. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as chilli oil is a plant-derived product; naturally zero. 11. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this is a non-hydrogenated plant oil product; confirmed zero by product category. 12. GI is estimated at 10 (low) by analogy with fat-dominant, low-carbohydrate condiments from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement exists for chilli oil. 13. The input food_group was listed as 'Grains and staples' which is incorrect for a chilli oil condiment; corrected to 'Oils, seasonings and condiments'. 14. Halal status is listed as false pending confirmation of slaughter certification and ingredient sourcing; Din Tai Fung Singapore outlets are not certified halal as of the knowledge cutoff. 15. The 160 g jar retail price of SGD 19.62 is preserved from the field survey source record for reference only and is not used as a nutritional data source.
